ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for hot chocolate beverage; not required for this food category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of hot chocolate (approximately 240 g) is a typical single serving for an adult.

nutrient_source.title: Hot Chocolate — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Hot Chocolate (cr_id D04020010). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 14191, Cocoa mix, prepared with whole milk) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: Cocoa-based milk beverage, GI value estimated from analogous food — Chocolate milk, GI ~34–40 (Atkinson et al., 2008)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for 'hot chocolate' prepared with cocoa powder, vanilla sauce, sugar and milk in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is estimated from the closest analogous food: chocolate milk (GI ~34–40, Atkinson FS et al., 'International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008', Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 16.6) / 100 = 6.7 for a 240 g serving (16.6 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar content (6.0 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g sugar-sweetened beverage threshold; sodium is low at 50 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit standard hot chocolate due to its added sugar content (6.0 g/100 g), which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; an unsweetened cocoa preparation with low-fat milk and no added sugar is a suitable alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar (6.0 g/100 g) and sugar-sweetened beverage classification; liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly and elevate postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid standard hot chocolate as a sugar-sweetened beverage (6.0 g sugar/100 g); if desired, substitute with unsweetened cocoa powder mixed with low-fat milk and no added sugar to retain cocoa flavonoid benefits without the glycaemic burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.4 g/100 g from whole milk) and added sugar (6.0 g/100 g) contribute to dyslipidaemia and endothelial dysfunction at regular intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, standard hot chocolate should be consumed sparingly due to its saturated fat and added sugar content; cocoa flavonoids in unsweetened preparations may offer cardioprotective benefits, but these are negated by the sugar and saturated fat in standard recipes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.0 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; cocoa polyphenols are beneficial but negated by sugar in standard preparation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer unsweetened cocoa with milk and no added sugar to obtain anti-inflammatory flavonoid benefits without the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot chocolate is a warm, sweetened beverage prepared by dissolving cocoa powder and vanilla sauce in hot milk with added sugar. It is a globally popular drink consumed across all age groups, particularly as a comforting snack or breakfast accompaniment. The beverage has a rich, mildly sweet chocolate flavour with a creamy texture derived from whole milk. Per 100 g, it provides 85 kcal, 3.99 g protein, 3.99 g fat (approximately 2.4 g saturated), 6.93 g carbohydrate, 6.02 g sugar, 1.08 g dietary fibre, 50 mg sodium, and 111 mg calcium. The calcium content from milk makes it a moderate source of this mineral. Cocoa powder contributes flavonoids (epicatechin, catechin) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, but these benefits are partially offset by the added sugar in standard preparations. Clinically, hot chocolate is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its sugar content exceeding 5 g/100 g, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard form. An unsweetened cocoa preparation with low-fat milk and no added sugar is recommended as a health-conscious alternative. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low), with a glycemic load of approximately 6.7 per standard 240 g serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in hot chocolate?
Is hot chocolate high in sugar?
Is hot chocolate good for blood pressure?
Hot cocoa vs hot chocolate difference
Can I drink hot chocolate if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D04020010, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central closest equivalent. GI estimated from peer-reviewed analogous food data (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). All values are internally consistent and plausible for a cocoa-milk beverage.
notes: Saturated fat (2.4 g/100 g) is an adapted estimate from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 14191, cocoa mix prepared with whole milk) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. Potassium (180 mg/100 g) is an adapted estimate from USDA FoodData Central for cocoa-milk beverage. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry, and no exact match available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific Singapore preparation as of 2026. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on chocolate milk (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for this specific hot chocolate preparation exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from input are retained as contextually valid (sodium 50 mg/100 g; energy 85 kcal/100 g). This food is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) for clinical purposes due to sugar content of 6.02 g/100 g exceeding the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold.
